title: Rotate Image
description: Rotate an n by n matrix by 90 degrees clockwise

approaches.name: Brute Force with Extra Space O(n²)
approaches.summary: Create a new matrix and copy elements from the original to their rotated positions using a formula. Use this for clarity when learning the rotation pattern, but it requires extra memory.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Rotate n×n Matrix 90° Clockwise Using Extra Space
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of rotating a square matrix by creating a new matrix and copying elements to rotated positions.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution using the brute force approach with extra space. The intuition is that rotating a matrix 90° clockwise transforms each element at position (i, j) in the original matrix to position (j, n-1-i) in the rotated matrix. This pattern emerges because rows become columns and columns become rows in reverse order. We create a new n×n matrix initialized with zeros, then iterate through every element of the original matrix, applying the transformation formula to determine its new position in the rotated matrix. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Determine n as the number of rows/columns. 2) Create a new n×n matrix result filled with zeros. 3) For i from 0 to n-1: For j from 0 to n-1: result[j][n-1-i] = matrix[i][j]. 4) Return result. Time complexity is O(n²) since we visit each of the n² elements exactly once. Space complexity is O(n²) for the new matrix. This approach is excellent for learning the rotation pattern clearly, though it's not optimal for memory. Common mistakes include: using incorrect indices in the transformation (e.g., result[i][j] = matrix[j][i] for transpose instead of rotation), forgetting that indices are zero-based, or not handling edge cases like n=0 or n=1 properly. The connection to L1 is that this level implements the complete solution using the key transformation logic from L1. Real code pattern: def rotate(matrix): n = len(matrix); rotated = [[0]*n for _ in range(n)]; for i in range(n): for j in range(n): rotated[j][n-1-i] = matrix[i][j]; return rotated.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Transform Element Position for 90° Clockwise Rotation
ladders.desc: Implement the core transformation that maps an element from (i,j) in original to (j,n-1-i) in rotated matrix.
ladders.explanation: This is the most important helper function in the brute force approach. The intuition is that rotating a matrix 90° clockwise involves two operations: transpose (rows become columns) and then reverse each row. However, in the brute force method, we directly compute the new position using the formula: for an element at position (i, j) in an n×n matrix, its position after rotation is (j, n-1-i). This works because: i represents the row index, j represents the column index. After rotation, the original row i becomes column n-1-i (counting from the right), and the original column j becomes row j. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Input: matrix, i, j, n. 2) Compute new_i = j. 3) Compute new_j = n-1-i. 4) Return new_i, new_j. Time complexity is O(1) per element. Space complexity is O(1). This sub-routine is the building block for the full solution at L0, where we apply it to every element. Common mistakes include: confusing clockwise with counterclockwise rotation (which would be (n-1-j, i)), forgetting that indices are zero-based (so n-1 is the last index), or incorrectly applying the formula (e.g., using n-i instead of n-1-i). The connection to L2 is that this implements the core logic of position mapping. Real code pattern: def transform_position(i, j, n): return j, n-1-i.

examples.trace: Step 1: Given i=0, j=0, n=3. Step 2: new_i = j = 0. Step 3: new_j = n-1-i = 3-1-0 = 2. Step 4: Return (0, 2). This means element at (0,0) moves to (0,2) after rotation.
Step 1: Given i=1, j=2, n=4. Step 2: new_i = j = 2. Step 3: new_j = n-1-i = 4-1-1 = 2. Step 4: Return (2, 2). This means element at (1,2) moves to (2,2) after rotation.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Mapping Matrix Indices Under 90° Rotation
ladders.desc: Understand and derive the formula that maps (i,j) to (j,n-1-i) for clockwise rotation.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight behind the brute force approach. The intuition comes from visualizing how coordinates change during rotation. Consider a point in a 2D grid: rotating 90° clockwise around the origin transforms (x,y) to (y,-x). For a matrix with indices (i,j), we need to adapt this to matrix coordinates where i is the row (vertical position) and j is the column (horizontal position). In matrix terms, i increases downward, and j increases rightward. After rotation, the new row index becomes the old column index (j), and the new column index becomes the complement of the old row index relative to the matrix size (n-1-i). This is because the top row (i=0) becomes the rightmost column (j=n-1), the second row (i=1) becomes the second-to-right column (j=n-2), and so on. Step-by-step derivation: 1) Original position: (i,j). 2) After 90° clockwise rotation, the element moves to a new position where the old row becomes a new column, and the old column becomes a new row. 3) The new row index is j (since columns become rows). 4) The new column index is n-1-i (since rows become columns, but reversed order because the top becomes right). Time/space analysis: This is a conceptual mapping, so O(1). This logic feeds into L1, where we implement it as a function. Common mistakes include: not accounting for zero-based indexing, confusing the direction of rotation, or misapplying the formula to non-square matrices (which this problem doesn't handle). The connection to L3 is that this builds on understanding matrix coordinates. Real code pattern: Concept only, no code.

examples.trace: Step 1: Original (i,j) = (0,0). Step 2: New row = j = 0. Step 3: New column = n-1-i = 3-1-0 = 2. Step 4: Mapped to (0,2). Visual: Top-left corner moves to top-right corner after rotation.
Step 1: Original (i,j) = (2,3). Step 2: New row = j = 3. Step 3: New column = n-1-i = 4-1-2 = 1. Step 4: Mapped to (3,1). Visual: Element in row 2, column 3 moves to row 3, column 1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Accessing and Assigning Elements in a 2D List
ladders.desc: Work with 2D lists (matrices) to get and set values at specific row and column indices.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for implementing the rotation. The intuition is that a 2D list in programming represents a matrix where each element is accessed using two indices: the first index selects the row, and the second selects the column within that row. For example, in Python, matrix[i][j] gives the element at row i, column j. To create a new matrix, we need to initialize a 2D list of appropriate size and assign values to specific positions. Step-by-step: 1) Accessing: Use matrix[i][j] to retrieve the value at position (i,j). 2) Assigning: Use matrix[i][j] = value to set the value at position (i,j). 3) Creating a new matrix: For an n×n matrix, use [[0]*n for _ in range(n)] to create a list of n rows, each with n zeros. Time complexity for access/assignment is O(1). Space complexity for creating a new matrix is O(n²). This feeds into L2, where we use these operations to apply the index mapping. Common mistakes include: confusing row and column indices (e.g., using matrix[j][i]), forgetting that lists are zero-indexed, or incorrectly initializing a 2D list (e.g., using [[0]*n]*n which creates shallow copies). The connection to L4 is that this builds on basic list operations. Real code pattern: Access: val = matrix[i][j]; Assign: matrix[i][j] = val; Create: new_matrix = [[0]*n for _ in range(n)].

examples.trace: Step 1: Matrix has rows: row0=[1,2], row1=[3,4]. Step 2: i=1 selects row1: [3,4]. Step 3: j=0 selects first element in row1: 3. Step 4: Return 3.

examples.input: Create 2×2 matrix, assign value 5 at i=0, j=1
examples.trace: Step 1: Create new matrix: [[0,0],[0,0]]. Step 2: i=0 selects first row: [0,0]. Step 3: j=1 selects second element in that row. Step 4: Assign 5: row becomes [0,5]. Step 5: Matrix is now [[0,5],[0,0]].

testCases.input: Create 3×3 matrix, assign 9 at i=2, j=2
Create 1×1 matrix, assign 7 at i=0, j=0
Create 2×3 matrix, assign x at i=1, j=0

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over a 2D List with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Use nested for-loops to traverse all elements in a 2D list (matrix).
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is used to visit every element in the matrix during rotation. The intuition is that a 2D list has rows and columns, so we need two loops: an outer loop for rows and an inner loop for columns. This ensures we process each element exactly once in row-major order (left to right, top to bottom). Step-by-step: 1) Determine n as the number of rows (which equals columns for a square matrix). 2) Outer loop: for i in range(n): iterates over row indices from 0 to n-1. 3) Inner loop: for j in range(n): iterates over column indices from 0 to n-1 for each row. 4) Inside the inner loop, access matrix[i][j] to do operations. Time complexity is O(n²) for an n×n matrix. Space complexity is O(1) if not creating new data structures. This feeds into L3, where we use iteration to access and assign elements. Common mistakes include: using incorrect range (e.g., range(1,n) instead of range(n)), confusing the order of loops, or not handling non-square matrices (but here n is same for rows and cols). The connection to L5 is that this builds on basic loop concepts. Real code pattern: for i in range(n): for j in range(n): # process matrix[i][j].

examples.trace: Step 1: n=2. Step 2: i=0: j=0: print matrix[0][0]=1; j=1: print matrix[0][1]=2. Step 3: i=1: j=0: print matrix[1][0]=3; j=1: print matrix[1][1]=4. Output: 1 2 3 4.

Step 1: n=2 rows, but cols=3 (non-square, but for illustration). Step 2: Initialize sum=0. Step 3: i=0: j=0: sum+=5→5; j=1: sum+=6→11; j=2: sum+=7→18. Step 4: i=1: j=0: sum+=8→26; j=1: sum+=9→35; j=2: sum+=10→45. Final sum=45.

testCases.input: matrix=[[1,0],[0,1]], iterate and check for ones

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding 2D Lists (Matrices) in Programming
ladders.desc: Learn what a 2D list is and how it represents rows and columns, starting from basic list concepts.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept for the rotation problem. A 2D list, or matrix, is a list of lists in programming. The intuition is that it mimics a grid or table with rows and columns. For example, [[1,2,3],[4,5,6]] represents a matrix with 2 rows and 3 columns: row 0 is [1,2,3] and row 1 is [4,5,6]. Each element is accessed using two indices: the first index picks the row (the outer list), and the second index picks the column within that row (the inner list). Key points: 1) Creation: matrix = [[1,2],[3,4]] creates a 2×2 matrix. 2) Access: matrix[0][1] gives the element at row 0, column 1 (value 2). 3) Dimensions: len(matrix) gives number of rows; len(matrix[0]) gives number of columns if all rows have same length. Time/space analysis: Access is O(1), storage is O(rows×cols). This foundation feeds into L4, where we iterate over such structures. Common mistakes include: thinking of indices as (column,row) instead of (row,column), or not understanding that matrix[i] returns a list (the i-th row). The connection to higher levels is that this is the basic data structure used in the problem. Real code pattern: matrix = [[1,2],[3,4]]; val = matrix[0][1] # val=2.

examples.trace: Step 1: Matrix has two rows: row0=[10,20,30], row1=[40,50,60]. Step 2: Row index 1 selects row1: [40,50,60]. Step 3: Column index 2 selects the third element in row1: 60. Step 4: Output 60.

examples.input: Create a 2×2 matrix with values a,b,c,d in row-major order
examples.trace: Step 1: A 2×2 matrix has 2 rows and 2 columns. Step 2: Row-major order means fill row 0 first: [a,b]. Step 3: Then row 1: [c,d]. Step 4: Combine: [[a,b],[c,d]].

testCases.input: matrix=[[1,2,3],[4,5,6]], what is the value at row 0, column 2?
matrix=[[x,y,z]], what is the number of columns?

approaches.name: Transpose and Reverse O(n²)
approaches.summary: First transpose the matrix by swapping elements across the diagonal, then reverse each row to achieve the 90-degree clockwise rotation. This is a common in-place method that leverages matrix properties.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Rotate n×n Matrix 90° Clockwise In-Place
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of matrix rotation using transpose and reverse operations.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires rotating an n×n matrix 90 degrees clockwise in-place without using extra space. The transpose-and-reverse approach works because a 90° clockwise rotation is mathematically equivalent to first transposing the matrix (swapping elements across the main diagonal) and then reversing each row. The intuition: transposing flips the matrix over its main diagonal, changing rows to columns. Reversing each row then completes the rotation. Step-by-step: 1. Transpose: For i from 0 to n-1, for j from i+1 to n-1, swap matrix[i][j] with matrix[j][i]. This ensures each element above the diagonal swaps with its symmetric counterpart below. 2. Reverse each row: For each row i, reverse the entire row from left to right. Time complexity is O(n²) since we visit each element once during transpose and once during reversal. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use constant extra space for swapping. Connection to L1: This combines the transpose operation (L1) with row reversal. Common mistakes include incorrect loop bounds during transpose (must start j from i+1 to avoid double-swapping), forgetting to handle odd-sized matrices correctly (they work fine), and misunderstanding in-place requirements. Real code pattern: def rotate(matrix): n = len(matrix); for i in range(n): for j in range(i+1, n): matrix[i][j], matrix[j][i] = matrix[j][i], matrix[i][j]; for i in range(n): matrix[i].reverse()

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Transpose an n×n Matrix In-Place
ladders.desc: Swap elements across the main diagonal to convert rows to columns.
ladders.explanation: Transposing a matrix means flipping it over its main diagonal, turning rows into columns. For an n×n matrix, element at position (i,j) moves to (j,i). The intuition: we only need to swap elements where i < j (above the diagonal) with their counterparts below the diagonal to avoid double-swapping. Step-by-step: 1. Iterate i from 0 to n-1. 2. For each i, iterate j from i+1 to n-1. 3. Swap matrix[i][j] with matrix[j][i]. This ensures each pair is swapped exactly once. Time complexity is O(n²) since we visit roughly n²/2 elements above the diagonal. Space complexity is O(1) as swaps are in-place. Connection to L0: This is the first half of the rotation solution; after transpose, reversing rows completes the rotation. Common mistakes include starting j from 0 (causes double-swapping and resets the matrix), incorrect bounds leading to index errors, or misunderstanding that transpose works for square matrices only in this context. Real code pattern: def transpose(matrix): n = len(matrix); for i in range(n): for j in range(i+1, n): matrix[i][j], matrix[j][i] = matrix[j][i], matrix[i][j]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Mathematical Insight: Transpose + Reverse = 90° Clockwise Rotation
ladders.desc: Understanding why transposing followed by row reversal rotates a matrix 90° clockwise.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that a 90-degree clockwise rotation of a matrix can be decomposed into two simpler operations: transpose and reverse. Intuition: In a matrix, rotating 90° clockwise moves element from position (i,j) to (j, n-1-i). Transposing swaps (i,j) with (j,i), effectively reflecting over the main diagonal. Then reversing each row swaps elements horizontally, mapping (j,i) to (j, n-1-i). Combined, this gives the desired rotation. Step-by-step reasoning: 1. Original position: (i,j). 2. After transpose: becomes (j,i). 3. After reversing row j: becomes (j, n-1-i). This matches the rotation formula. This works for any square matrix size. Time/space analysis: The insight itself doesn't involve computation, but enables O(n²) time and O(1) space algorithm. Connection to L1: This explains why the transpose sub-routine (L1) is crucial. Common mistakes include confusing clockwise vs counterclockwise (reverse columns instead of rows for counterclockwise), or not verifying with examples. Real code pattern: This is conceptual, but can be expressed as: rotation(matrix) = reverse_rows(transpose(matrix)).

examples.trace: Original: matrix=[[1,2],[3,4]], element 2 at (0,1). Rotation goal: 90° clockwise moves it to (1,0) with value 3? Wait, let's trace properly. After full rotation: [[3,1],[4,2]], so element from (0,1)=2 goes to (1,0)=2? Actually in rotated matrix, position (1,0) has 4, not 2. Let's recalc: Original (0,1)=2. Rotation formula: new position = (j, n-1-i) = (1, 2-1-0) = (1,1). In rotated matrix [[3,1],[4,2]], (1,1)=2. Yes. Now apply insight: Step 1 - Transpose: (0,1) swaps with (1,0), so 2 moves to (1,0). Step 2 - Reverse row 1: row 1 is [3,2] (after transpose), reverse to [2,3], so element at (1,0)=2 moves to (1,1)=2. Matches rotation.

Original: matrix=[[1,2,3],[4,5,6],[7,8,9]], element 6 at (1,2). Rotation goal: new position = (j, n-1-i) = (2, 3-1-1) = (2,1). In rotated matrix [[7,4,1],[8,5,2],[9,6,3]], (2,1)=6. Apply insight: Step 1 - Transpose: (1,2) swaps with (2,1), so 6 moves to (2,1). Step 2 - Reverse row 2: row 2 is [3,6,9] (after transpose), reverse to [9,6,3], so element at (2,1)=6 stays at (2,1)=6. Matches rotation.

ladders.title: Building Block — Reverse a List In-Place
ladders.desc: Flip the order of elements in a list without using extra space.
ladders.explanation: Reversing a list in-place means modifying the original list so its elements appear in opposite order, without allocating a new list. The intuition: swap the first element with the last, second with second-last, etc., meeting in the middle. Step-by-step: 1. Use two pointers: left starting at index 0, right starting at index len(list)-1. 2. While left < right, swap list[left] and list[right]. 3. Increment left and decrement right. This ensures each pair is swapped once. Time complexity is O(n) for a list of length n, as we visit each element once. Space complexity is O(1) since we only use constant extra variables for pointers and swapping. Connection to L2: This operation is the 'reverse' part in the core logic for matrix rotation, applied to each row. Common mistakes include using while left <= right (covers middle element unnecessarily), incorrect pointer updates, or trying to create a new list which violates in-place requirement. Real code pattern: def reverse_in_place(lst): left, right = 0, len(lst)-1; while left < right: lst[left], lst[right] = lst[right], lst[left]; left += 1; right -= 1

examples.trace: Start: [1,2,3,4,5], left=0, right=4. Swap lst[0]=1 with lst[4]=5 → [5,2,3,4,1]; left=1, right=3. Swap lst[1]=2 with lst[3]=4 → [5,4,3,2,1]; left=2, right=2, loop stops as left < right is false. Done.
Start: [10,20,30,40], left=0, right=3. Swap lst[0]=10 with lst[3]=40 → [40,20,30,10]; left=1, right=2. Swap lst[1]=20 with lst[2]=30 → [40,30,20,10]; left=2, right=1, loop stops. Done.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Swap Two Elements In-Place
ladders.desc: Exchange the values of two variables without using temporary storage.
ladders.explanation: Swapping two elements in-place means interchanging their values without allocating extra memory for a temporary variable. The intuition: use arithmetic or tuple unpacking to exchange values directly. Step-by-step methods: 1. Using a temporary variable (common but uses extra space): temp = a; a = b; b = temp. 2. Without temporary variable (true in-place): use arithmetic for numbers, e.g., a = a + b; b = a - b; a = a - b. 3. In Python, tuple unpacking achieves this elegantly: a, b = b, a. For matrix operations, we typically use tuple unpacking as it's clear and efficient. Time complexity is O(1) as it's a constant-time operation. Space complexity is O(1) for tuple unpacking (Python creates a temporary tuple internally, but it's negligible and considered in-place in practice). Connection to L3: This operation is used repeatedly in reversing a list (swapping elements) and in transposing a matrix (swapping across diagonal). Common mistakes include incorrect order in arithmetic swaps leading to data loss, or misunderstanding in-place constraints. Real code pattern: For variables a and b: a, b = b, a

examples.trace: Start: a=5, b=10. Using tuple unpacking: a, b = b, a. First, Python evaluates right side: (b, a) = (10, 5). Then assigns: a = 10, b = 5. Done.
Start: x=-3, y=7. Using tuple unpacking: x, y = y, x. Right side: (y, x) = (7, -3). Assign: x = 7, y = -3. Done.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Accessing Elements in a 2D List (Matrix)
ladders.desc: Understanding how to read and write elements in a nested list representing a matrix.
ladders.explanation: A 2D list in programming is a list of lists, often used to represent a matrix. Each inner list is a row, and elements are accessed using two indices: matrix[row][column]. The intuition: think of it as a grid where the first index picks the row (vertical position), and the second picks the column (horizontal position). Step-by-step: 1. Declaration: matrix = [[1,2,3],[4,5,6],[7,8,9]] creates a 3x3 matrix. 2. Access: matrix[0][0] gives 1 (top-left), matrix[1][2] gives 6 (second row, third column). 3. Modification: matrix[0][1] = 10 changes the element at row 0, column 1 to 10. Time/space analysis: Access is O(1) constant time. Connection to L4: This foundational skill is needed to perform swaps (L4) on matrix elements. Common mistakes include confusing row and column order (always row first), index out-of-bounds errors (indices start at 0), or misunderstanding nested structure. Real code pattern: To iterate through all elements: for i in range(len(matrix)): for j in range(len(matrix[i])): print(matrix[i][j])

examples.input: matrix=[[1,2],[3,4]], access element at row 1, column 0
examples.trace: Matrix is: row 0: [1,2], row 1: [3,4]. Access matrix[1][0]: First index 1 selects row 1: [3,4]. Second index 0 selects first element of that row: 3. So output is 3.

examples.input: matrix=[[5,6,7],[8,9,10]], modify element at row 0, column 2 to 100
examples.trace: Start: matrix=[[5,6,7],[8,9,10]]. Modify matrix[0][2]: row 0 is [5,6,7], column 2 is third element (index 2), currently 7. Change it to 100: row 0 becomes [5,6,100]. Matrix is now [[5,6,100],[8,9,10]].
